Is anyone sure of this answer?
Lower pressure = stronger storm? Is that correct?
Thank you Kayla and Alex!
yes. The more the pressure drops, the stronger it gets.
yes

correct. Nature wants equilibrium. Air rushes into areas of low pressure (storms are low pressure systems. That’s why there is wind… high pressure air rushing into low pressure.) The lower the pressure, the more air that rushes in and the stronger the storm. Think about a fish tank with a divider. One side of the tank is full of water and the other is empty. Remove the divider, and the water rushes in until the level is equal. Air does the same thing. Hurricanes are just incredibly strong storms driven by low pressure. Warm ocean water heats the air at the surface which rises, causing areas of low pressure (the empty side of the fish tank). High pressure air around the storm rushes in… creating hurricane force winds. There is obviously more to it than that, but I hope it helps. This is why hurricanes are stronger when the surface water is warmer. The job of a hurricane is to bleed heat off the ocean. The warmer the ocean, the larger the storm needed to cool it down. Hurricanes are actually an important mechanism by which the Earth cools the ocean. Sadly, we just happen to be in the way
